original_abstract: Extracting structured knowledge from scientific literature remains a significant challenge for machine learning models due to the density and complexity of the text. In this work, we present a straightforward approach to joint named entity recognition and relation extraction by fine-tuning pretrained large language models, specifically GPT-3 and Llama-2, to identify and categorize complex scientific data. We evaluate this method across three representative tasks in materials chemistry: linking dopants to host materials, cataloging metal-organic frameworks, and extracting general information regarding material compositions, phases, morphologies, and applications. Our models process information from both individual sentences and full paragraphs, yielding outputs in flexible formats ranging from natural language sentences to structured JSON objects. This methodology offers a highly accessible and versatile route for building large-scale, specialized databases of structured knowledge directly from research papers.
humanized_abstract: Extracting meaningful information from scientific literature is still a challenging task for machine learning models due to the high density and complexity of scientific texts. Here we show a very simple method for performing joint named entity recognition and relation extraction using pre-trained large language models such as GPT-3 and Llama-2. The models are fine-tuned for identifying and classifying complex scientific information in materials chemistry, which is demonstrated on three benchmark tasks: 1) Dopants and host materials, 2) Metal-organic frameworks, and 3) Material composition, phase, morphology and application. Excluding prior knowledge about the specific scientific literature, our models can extract meaningful information from both individual sentences as well as full paragraphs and output the results in various formats such as individual sentences, lists, tables, and even in the form of JSON objects. Overall, our method presents a very straightforward way to populate large databases of structured knowledge from scientific literature for various specific applications.
